description: Comprehensive database of known AI code generation failure patterns. Used by the /preflight plugin to detect hallucinated packages, non-existent APIs, plausible-but-wrong logic, deprecated patterns, unsafe type assertions, and missing error handling.

critical.label: Critical
critical.description: Will cause build failure, runtime crash, or security vulnerability. Must be fixed before merge.

high.label: High
high.description: Will cause incorrect behavior or subtle bugs that are hard to detect in review. Should be fixed before merge.

medium.label: Medium
medium.description: May cause issues in certain environments or is a maintenance concern. Should be flagged for review.

low.label: Low
low.description: Style issue or minor concern. Informational flag.

categories.label: Phantom Package
categories.description: Packages that AI models commonly hallucinate. These packages either never existed, were renamed, or are plausible-sounding variants of real packages.
categories.why_ai_does_this: LLMs interpolate between real package names in training data, generating plausible but non-existent variants. They also confuse old package names with current ones, or invent convenience wrappers that don't exist.

categories.label: Hallucinated API
categories.description: API methods, properties, or function signatures that do not exist on the referenced library or built-in.
categories.why_ai_does_this: LLMs blend APIs from different versions, different libraries, or entirely fabricate methods whose names sound correct based on the library's naming conventions.

categories.label: Plausible Wrong Logic
categories.description: Code that looks correct at a glance but contains subtle logical errors that produce wrong results.
categories.why_ai_does_this: LLMs optimize for plausible-looking code rather than correct code. They frequently produce off-by-one errors, inverted conditions, and swapped arguments because the wrong version looks equally natural in context.

categories.label: Deprecated API
categories.description: APIs that existed in older versions of libraries or runtimes but have been deprecated or removed. The AI's training data contained heavy usage of these patterns.
categories.why_ai_does_this: Training data is dominated by older code. Deprecated patterns appear far more frequently in the training corpus than their modern replacements, causing the model to prefer them.

categories.label: Confident Wrong Types
categories.description: Type assertions, casts, or generic usage that bypasses the type system without validation, hiding potential runtime errors.
categories.why_ai_does_this: When the AI encounters a type error during generation, it reaches for type assertions (as, any, !) to silence the error rather than fixing the underlying type mismatch. This is the path of least resistance in autoregressive generation.

categories.label: Missing Error Handling
categories.description: Code paths where errors, rejections, or edge cases are silently ignored, violating project conventions or best practices.
categories.why_ai_does_this: LLMs generate the 'happy path' first and often fail to add error handling because the training data frequently shows examples without it, and error handling adds complexity that reduces generation fluency.

categories.label: Async Mistakes
categories.description: Incorrect async/await usage, unhandled promises, race conditions, and parallelism errors that cause silent failures, data loss, or unpredictable behavior.
categories.why_ai_does_this: LLMs generate async code that looks syntactically correct but violates JavaScript's concurrency model. They treat forEach as if it respects await, forget to await calls when the return value is used downstream, and parallelize operations that have ordering dependencies because the parallel version reads more cleanly.

categories.label: React Mistakes
categories.description: Violations of React's rules of hooks, incorrect component lifecycle patterns, and misuse of React APIs that cause infinite loops, stale data, or hydration mismatches.
categories.why_ai_does_this: LLMs generate code that appears to follow React patterns but violates the rules of hooks (conditional calls, missing dependencies) because the training data contains many examples of these mistakes in tutorials and Stack Overflow answers. The model also confuses Server Component and Client Component constraints.

categories.label: Next.js Mistakes
categories.description: Confusion between Pages Router and App Router patterns, incorrect use of Next.js-specific APIs, and version-specific breaking changes that cause build failures or runtime errors.
categories.why_ai_does_this: LLMs conflate Pages Router and App Router conventions because both are heavily represented in training data. They also lag behind Next.js version changes, especially the async request APIs introduced in Next.js 15 and the evolving Server Component model.

categories.label: Database Mistakes
categories.description: N+1 queries, SQL injection, missing transactions, non-deterministic queries, and connection management errors that cause performance degradation, data corruption, or security vulnerabilities.
categories.why_ai_does_this: LLMs generate database code that works for a single request in development but fails at scale. They default to the simplest query pattern (individual queries in a loop) rather than batched or joined queries, and treat database operations as infallible single-step actions that need no transactional safety.

categories.label: API Design Mistakes
categories.description: HTTP API anti-patterns including incorrect status codes, missing headers, security misconfigurations, and scalability oversights that cause integration issues and vulnerabilities.
categories.why_ai_does_this: LLMs generate API endpoints that work in a happy-path demo but violate HTTP semantics and production best practices. They return 200 for errors because it is the simplest path, omit pagination because the training examples rarely show large datasets, and skip rate limiting because it is infrastructure-level concern that rarely appears in code samples.

patterns.description: Hallucinated npm packages that do not exist or have been renamed

detection_strategy.method: Check each imported/required package against the npm registry. Flag any package that returns 404 or has zero downloads.

heuristics: Package name is a real package name with a suffix like -v2, -v3, -lite, -mini, -utils, -helper, -pro, -next
Package name uses @ scope that doesn't match the library's actual scope
Package was recently renamed but AI uses the old name
Package name combines two real package names

npm.notes: Zod has no 'mini' variant. Use zod directly.
Zod has no 'lite' variant.
No official zod-utils package. Utility functions are built into zod.
express-validator does not publish version-suffixed packages.
Renamed to @tanstack/react-query in v4. The old react-query package is deprecated.
No lodash-utils package exists. Use lodash or lodash-es.
node-fetch v3 is published under the same node-fetch name, not node-fetch-v3.
The correct scoped package is @prisma/client, not prisma-client.
next-auth v5 is published as next-auth, not next-auth-v5.
tailwind-merge v2 is published under the same package name.
No version-suffixed variant exists.
Mongoose publishes all versions under the mongoose package name.
No version-suffixed variant. Consider date-fns or luxon instead of moment.
v6 is published as react-router-dom, not react-router-v6.
react-helmet is unmaintained. react-helmet-async is the active fork.
No version-suffixed package exists.
next/router is a built-in Next.js module, not a separate package.
next/image is a built-in Next.js component, not a separate package.
No webpack-utils package. Webpack utilities are in webpack or separate named plugins.
babel-core is the Babel 6 package. Babel 7+ uses @babel/core.
Babel 7+ uses scoped packages under @babel/.
No version-suffixed variant.
graphql-tools was split into scoped packages under @graphql-tools/.
No typeorm-utils package exists.
Fastify plugins moved to @fastify/ scope in Fastify v4.
Moved to @fastify/ scope.
Moved to @fastify/ scope.
Moved to @fastify/ scope.
NestJS uses @nestjs/ scoped packages.
NestJS uses @nestjs/ scoped packages.
No sharp-utils package.
No version-suffixed variant.
chalk v5 is ESM-only but published under the same name.
Use uuid package and import { v4 } from 'uuid'. No uuid-v4 package.
dotenv-safe does exist but is unmaintained. AI often suggests it when dotenv alone suffices.
Node.js built-in crypto module. No crypto-utils package needed.
No version-suffixed variant.
Published under the same name for all versions.

hallucination_name_patterns.description: Real package name with version suffix (e.g., express-v5, lodash-v4)
Real package name with size-reduction suffix
Real package name with utility suffix
Real package name with premium/extended suffix
Real package name with 'newer version' suffix
Prefixing 'node-' to a built-in or well-known package

examples.bad: import { z } from 'zod-mini';
examples.good: import { z } from 'zod';
examples.explanation: zod-mini does not exist. Zod is already lightweight. Use zod directly.

examples.bad: import { useQuery } from 'react-query';
examples.good: import { useQuery } from '@tanstack/react-query';
examples.explanation: react-query was renamed to @tanstack/react-query in v4.

examples.bad: const { PrismaClient } = require('prisma-client');
examples.good: const { PrismaClient } = require('@prisma/client');
examples.explanation: The correct package is @prisma/client (scoped package).

patterns.description: Hallucinated Python packages that do not exist or have been renamed

detection_strategy.method: Check each imported package against PyPI. Flag any package that returns 404 or has negligible downloads.

heuristics: Package name is a real package with a -utils or -helpers suffix
Package combines two real library names
Package uses old naming convention (pre-rename)
Package sounds like a convenience wrapper that doesn't exist

pypi.notes: The import is 'import sklearn' but the pip package is scikit-learn.
The import is 'import cv2' but the pip package is opencv-python.
The import is 'import yaml' but the pip package is pyyaml.
The import is 'from dotenv import load_dotenv' but pip package is python-dotenv.
No flask-utils package. Flask extensions use flask-* naming.
django.utils is a built-in module, not a separate package.
No pandas-utils package exists.
No numpy-utils package exists.
torch.utils is a built-in submodule, not a separate package.
TF Lite runtime is tflite-runtime, not tensorflow-lite.
A fastapi-utils package does exist but is often confused with fastapi's built-in utilities. Verify which is needed.
Pydantic v2 is published as pydantic. No version-suffixed variant.
No version-suffixed variant.
No celery-utils package.
requests-async was deprecated. Use httpx for async HTTP.
No aiohttp-utils package.
No boto3-utils package.
No pytest-utils. Pytest plugins use pytest-* naming with specific names.
No pillow-utils. Pillow is the PIL fork.
No scrapy-utils package.

examples.bad: pip install sklearn
examples.good: pip install scikit-learn
examples.explanation: The pip package name is scikit-learn, not sklearn. The import name differs from the package name.

examples.bad: pip install cv2
examples.good: pip install opencv-python
examples.explanation: The pip package is opencv-python. cv2 is only the import name.

examples.bad: pip install dotenv
examples.good: pip install python-dotenv
examples.explanation: The pip package is python-dotenv. The import name 'dotenv' differs from the package name.

patterns.description: Non-existent methods on JavaScript/TypeScript built-in objects

detection_strategy.method: Parse method calls on known types and verify against the actual API surface. Use TypeScript compiler API or a known-methods database.

heuristics: Method name sounds correct but doesn't exist on the type
Method exists on a different type (e.g., Array method used on Object)
Method is from a proposal that was never adopted
Method signature has wrong parameter order

known_hallucinations.notes: Array.prototype.flatten was proposed but shipped as Array.prototype.flat.
flatMap does exist but AI often uses wrong signature. It takes (element, index) => element, not (element) => [element].
No .last property on arrays. Use .at(-1) or arr[arr.length - 1].
No .first property on arrays. Use .at(0) or arr[0].
No .compact() on arrays. This is a lodash method. Use .filter(Boolean).
No .unique() on arrays. Use [...new Set(arr)] or lodash.uniq.
No .isEmpty() on arrays. Check .length === 0.
Object has no static .map method. Use Object.entries(obj).map().
Object has no static .filter method.
Object has no static .forEach method.
String.prototype.contains was renamed to .includes before shipping.
No .capitalize() on strings. This is a Python/lodash method.
No .reverse() on strings. Split to array first.
Promise.delay is from Bluebird, not native Promises.
Promise.map is from Bluebird. Use Promise.all(arr.map(fn)).
Promise.props is from Bluebird, not native.
Map has no .map() method. Convert to array first.
Map has no .filter() method. Convert to array first.
No JSON.clone. Use structuredClone() or JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(obj)).
No Number.clamp or Math.clamp. Use Math.min(Math.max(val, min), max).

examples.bad: const items = myArray.flatten();
examples.good: const items = myArray.flat();
examples.explanation: Array.prototype.flatten does not exist. The method was renamed to .flat() before it shipped.

examples.bad: if (name.contains('admin')) { ... }
examples.good: if (name.includes('admin')) { ... }
examples.explanation: String.prototype.contains does not exist. Use .includes() instead.

examples.bad: const mapped = Object.map(config, (key, val) => val.toUpperCase());
examples.good: const mapped = Object.fromEntries(Object.entries(config).map(([key, val]) => [key, val.toUpperCase()]));
examples.explanation: Object has no static .map() method. Use Object.entries() with Array.map().

patterns.description: Non-existent or confused APIs on popular npm libraries

detection_strategy.method: Cross-reference method calls with the library's actual TypeScript definitions or documentation.

known_hallucinations.notes: Express .listen takes positional args, not an options object. Fastify uses the object form.
res.json sends the body. Use res.status() to set status code separately.
AI often forgets that req.body requires express.json() or body-parser middleware.
In Next.js App Router, useRouter() no longer has .query. Use useSearchParams() instead.
App Router's router.push only accepts a string URL, not an object with pathname and query.
createRef is for class components. Function components should use the useRef hook.
Unlike class setState, useState's setter does not accept a callback second argument.
Prisma has no plain .find() method. Use findUnique, findFirst, or findMany.
Prisma update always requires a where clause.

known_hallucinations.real_api: findByIdAndUpdate returns a Query, lean() works but behavior is subtle
known_hallucinations.notes: AI often chains .lean() on update operations where it has no meaningful effect on the returned document.
Zod schemas use .parse() or .safeParse(), not .validate().
Order matters in Zod chains. min/max should come before refinements like email.
axios.post takes data as second arg, config as third. AI often puts config in second position.
jwt.decode does NOT verify the signature. Always use jwt.verify for auth.

examples.bad:
const router = useRouter();
const { id } = router.query;
examples.explanation: In Next.js App Router, useRouter() from next/navigation has no .query property. Use useParams() for path params or useSearchParams() for query params.

examples.bad: const user = await prisma.user.find({ email: 'a@b.com' });
examples.good: const user = await prisma.user.findUnique({ where: { email: 'a@b.com' } });
examples.explanation: Prisma has no .find() method. Use findUnique (requires unique field) or findFirst.

examples.bad:
const decoded = jwt.decode(token);
if (decoded.userId) { /* grant access */ }
examples.good:
const decoded = jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET);
if (decoded.userId) { /* grant access */ }
examples.explanation: jwt.decode does NOT verify the token signature. Anyone can craft a token that decodes successfully. Always use jwt.verify for authentication.

patterns.description: Non-existent or confused Python standard library and popular package APIs

detection_strategy.method: Parse method calls and verify against known Python API surfaces using AST analysis or a known-methods database.

known_hallucinations.notes: dict.has_key() was removed in Python 3. Use 'key in dict'.
No .contains() method on lists. Use 'item in list'.
No .to_int() on strings. Use int() built-in.
No .to_float() on strings. Use float() built-in.
No .is_empty(). Use truthiness check: 'if not s:'.
os.path has no .read() method. Use open() or pathlib.
No .to_json_records(). Use to_json(orient='records').
No .filter_by() on DataFrames. That is SQLAlchemy syntax.
FastAPI uses @app.get(), @app.post(), etc., not @app.route() like Flask.
.all() returns a list, so .filter() cannot be chained after it.
In Pydantic v2, .dict() is deprecated. Use .model_dump().
In Pydantic v2, .parse_obj() is deprecated. Use .model_validate().

examples.bad: if my_dict.has_key('name'):
examples.good: if 'name' in my_dict:
examples.explanation: dict.has_key() was removed in Python 3. Use the 'in' operator.

examples.bad: data = user.dict()
examples.good: data = user.model_dump()
examples.explanation: In Pydantic v2, .dict() is deprecated. Use .model_dump() instead.

patterns.description: Off-by-one errors in loops, slices, and boundary conditions

detection_strategy.method: Analyze loop bounds, slice operations, and array indexing for common off-by-one patterns.

heuristics: Loop uses <= where < is correct (or vice versa)
Slice end index is off by one
Array index uses .length instead of .length - 1
Pagination offset calculation is wrong
Range is exclusive when inclusive was intended

known_patterns.description: Using <= array.length in a for loop (accesses index out of bounds)
Wrong end index in slice operations
known_patterns.notes: Requires semantic understanding. Slice end is exclusive in both JS and Python.

known_patterns.description: Pagination offset miscalculation (page * size vs (page - 1) * size)
known_patterns.notes: If pages are 1-indexed, offset should be (page - 1) * size. AI often uses page * size.

known_patterns.description: Using .substring with wrong indices
Counting items vs counting gaps between items
known_patterns.notes: E.g., number of segments = number of dividers + 1. AI frequently gets this wrong.

examples.explanation: Using <= arr.length causes an access at arr[arr.length] which is undefined. Use < for zero-based indexing.

examples.bad:
const offset = page * pageSize;
const results = await db.query().skip(offset).limit(pageSize);
examples.good:
const offset = (page - 1) * pageSize;
const results = await db.query().skip(offset).limit(pageSize);
examples.explanation: If pages are 1-indexed (page 1 = first page), the offset must be (page - 1) * pageSize. Using page * pageSize skips the first page.

patterns.description: Inverted boolean conditions and negation errors

detection_strategy.method: Identify conditional expressions where the logic may be inverted relative to the described intent.

heuristics: Guard clause negation is inverted (should return early on failure, not success)
Auth check allows instead of denies
Null check is backwards
isEmpty check is inverted

known_patterns.description: Auth check that allows unauthenticated users instead of blocking them
known_patterns.notes: Guard clauses should check the NEGATIVE case: if (!isAuthenticated) return 401. AI often inverts this.

known_patterns.description: Null check that proceeds when null instead of when non-null
known_patterns.notes: Early returns should trigger on the null/undefined case: if (!value) return null.

known_patterns.description: Feature flag check that shows feature when disabled
known_patterns.notes: if (featureDisabled) { showFeature() } -- AI confuses positive and negative flag names.

examples.explanation: The guard clause should block unauthenticated users. Without the negation, it blocks authenticated users instead.
The null check is inverted. It throws 'not found' when the user IS found.

patterns.description: Swapped arguments in security-sensitive function calls

detection_strategy.method: Check argument order for known security-sensitive functions where swapping creates vulnerabilities.

heuristics: bcrypt.compare with hash and plaintext swapped
crypto.timingSafeEqual with attacker-controlled value in wrong position
JWT sign with payload and secret swapped
HMAC with key and data swapped

known_patterns.description: bcrypt.compare arguments swapped: hash first, plaintext second
known_patterns.notes: bcrypt.compare takes (plaintext, hash). AI frequently puts the hash first.

known_patterns.description: HMAC created with data as key and key as data
known_patterns.notes: createHmac second arg is the key, not the data. Data goes in .update().

known_patterns.description: timingSafeEqual with user input as first argument
known_patterns.notes: While functionally equivalent, convention is expected first for clarity.

known_patterns.description: jwt.sign with secret in payload position
known_patterns.notes: AI sometimes puts the secret or options object in the payload position.

known_patterns.description: Python bcrypt.checkpw with hash and password swapped
known_patterns.notes: bcrypt.checkpw takes (password, hashed). AI often puts hashed first.

examples.bad: const isValid = await bcrypt.compare(user.passwordHash, plainTextPassword);
examples.good: const isValid = await bcrypt.compare(plainTextPassword, user.passwordHash);
examples.explanation: bcrypt.compare takes (plaintext, hash). Swapping the arguments may not throw an error but will always return false, locking all users out.

examples.bad:
const hmac = crypto.createHmac('sha256', requestBody);
hmac.update(secret);
examples.good:
const hmac = crypto.createHmac('sha256', secret);
hmac.update(requestBody);
examples.explanation: createHmac takes the key as second argument. The data to sign goes in .update(). Swapping them produces a valid HMAC but with wrong semantics -- anyone with the data could forge signatures.

patterns.description: Wrong comparison operators and equality checks

detection_strategy.method: Identify comparisons that use the wrong operator type or compare incompatible values.

known_patterns.description: Using == instead of === in JavaScript
known_patterns.notes: Loose equality causes type coercion bugs: '0' == false is true, '' == 0 is true, null == undefined is true.

known_patterns.description: Using === to compare objects or arrays (always false for different references)
known_patterns.notes: AI often writes arr1 === arr2 or obj1 === obj2 expecting deep equality.

known_patterns.description: Comparing a string to a number without conversion
known_patterns.notes: '10' > '9' is false in JS because string comparison is lexicographic.

known_patterns.description: Using 'is' instead of '==' for value comparison in Python
known_patterns.notes: 'is' checks identity, not equality. Works for None/True/False but not general values. AI often writes 'if x is 0' or 'if s is ""'.

known_patterns.description: Exact equality comparison of floating point numbers
known_patterns.notes: 0.1 + 0.2 === 0.3 is false. Use Math.abs(a - b) < epsilon.

examples.explanation: Loose equality (==) means '' == 0 is true, '0' == 0 is true, false == 0 is true. Use strict equality (===).

examples.bad:
if (result is 200):
    print('success')
examples.good:
if result == 200:
    print('success')
examples.explanation: Python 'is' checks object identity, not value equality. This may work for small integers due to CPython caching but is unreliable.

patterns.description: Async/await mistakes and unhandled promise patterns

detection_strategy.method: Analyze async functions for missing awaits, unhandled rejections, and incorrect async patterns.

known_patterns.description: Calling an async function without await
known_patterns.notes: AI often drops await, especially in conditionals or when the result is used later.

known_patterns.description: Using await inside Array.forEach (does not wait for completion)
known_patterns.notes: forEach does not await the callback. Use for...of loop or Promise.all(arr.map(...)).

known_patterns.description: Using await inside .filter() or .map() without Promise.all
known_patterns.notes: .filter(async fn) always returns all items because async functions return truthy promises. .map(async fn) returns Promise[], not resolved values.

known_patterns.description: Unnecessary return await in non-try-catch context
known_patterns.notes: return await is unnecessary unless inside a try-catch block. Minor issue but indicates AI pattern.

known_patterns.description: Making a class constructor async (not allowed)
known_patterns.notes: Constructors cannot be async. Use a static async factory method instead.

known_patterns.description: Wrapping an existing promise in a new Promise constructor
known_patterns.notes: If you already have a promise, don't wrap it in new Promise. Just return/await it directly.

examples.explanation: forEach does not await async callbacks. The loop fires all promises and immediately moves on. Use for...of for sequential or Promise.all for parallel.
filter() with an async callback returns ALL items because async functions return Promise objects, which are truthy. You must resolve the promises first.

patterns.description: Deprecated React patterns from AI training data

detection_strategy.method: Scan for known deprecated React lifecycle methods, APIs, and patterns.

known_deprecations.replacement: useEffect with empty deps or constructor logic in useState initializer
known_deprecations.notes: Unsafe lifecycle. Use useEffect or useState initializer.

known_deprecations.replacement: useEffect with dependency array or getDerivedStateFromProps
known_deprecations.notes: Unsafe lifecycle. Usually a sign of derived state antipattern.
Unsafe lifecycle.

known_deprecations.replacement: class Component extends React.Component or function component
known_deprecations.notes: Removed entirely. AI still generates this occasionally.
React 18 uses createRoot API. ReactDOM.render disables concurrent features.
Moved to separate prop-types package, but TypeScript is now preferred.
Use ref forwarding and useRef instead.
Use default values in parameter destructuring: function Comp({ size = 'md' }).
In React 19, ref is passed as a regular prop. forwardRef is no longer needed.
The Consumer render prop pattern is deprecated in favor of the useContext hook.

examples.bad: ReactDOM.render(<App />, document.getElementById('root'));
examples.good:
const root = ReactDOM.createRoot(document.getElementById('root'));
root.render(<App />);
examples.explanation: ReactDOM.render is deprecated in React 18. Use createRoot for concurrent features.
defaultProps on function components is deprecated in React 18.3 and removed in React 19. Use JavaScript default parameters.

patterns.description: Deprecated Next.js patterns from AI training data

detection_strategy.method: Detect Next.js patterns that are obsolete in the App Router paradigm.

known_deprecations.replacement: async Server Components or route handlers
known_deprecations.context: Only deprecated if file is in the app/ directory. Still valid in pages/.
known_deprecations.notes: AI frequently mixes Pages Router and App Router patterns.

known_deprecations.replacement: async Server Components with fetch + revalidate
known_deprecations.context: Only deprecated if file is in the app/ directory. Still valid in pages/.
known_deprecations.notes: Use fetch() with { next: { revalidate: seconds } } in Server Components.

known_deprecations.context: Only deprecated if file is in the app/ directory.
known_deprecations.notes: Use export async function generateStaticParams() in App Router.

known_deprecations.replacement: Metadata API (export const metadata or generateMetadata)
known_deprecations.context: Deprecated in app/ directory. Still valid in pages/.
known_deprecations.notes: Use export const metadata = {} or export async function generateMetadata().
useRouter from next/navigation has a different API. No .query, .asPath, etc.
App Router uses layout.tsx instead of _app.tsx for shared layouts.
App Router uses the root layout.tsx to define html/body structure.
Next.js 15 supports TypeScript config. AI often generates CJS config.

known_deprecations.deprecated: middleware.ts using NextResponse.rewrite for API proxying
known_deprecations.replacement: Route Handlers or rewrites in next.config
known_deprecations.notes: AI often suggests overly complex middleware when simpler solutions exist.

examples.explanation: getServerSideProps does not work in the App Router. Server Components can fetch data directly in the component body.
next/head does not work in App Router. Use the Metadata API instead.

patterns.description: Deprecated Node.js built-in APIs

detection_strategy.method: Scan for known deprecated Node.js API calls.

known_deprecations.notes: Security issue: new Buffer(number) creates uninitialized memory. Buffer.alloc() zeros memory.
Same security issue as new Buffer().
url.parse has known vulnerabilities with certain inputs. Use the WHATWG URL API.
Use URLSearchParams from the URL module.
fs.exists is deprecated due to race conditions. Use fs.access or try/catch with fs.stat.
In ESM, use import.meta.dirname (Node 21+) or import.meta.url with fileURLToPath.
createCipher uses weak key derivation. Always use createCipheriv with a proper IV.
The domain module is deprecated. Use structured error handling or async_hooks.
AI sometimes sets NODE_ENV at runtime. Many libraries read it once at import time.
Use stream.pipeline or stream.promises.pipeline.
Ancient deprecation but AI occasionally generates it.

examples.bad: const buf = new Buffer(userInput);
examples.good: const buf = Buffer.from(userInput, 'utf-8');
examples.explanation: new Buffer() is deprecated and has a security vulnerability: new Buffer(100) returns uninitialized memory that may contain sensitive data.

examples.bad:
const parsed = url.parse(req.url, true);
const id = parsed.query.id;
examples.good:
const parsed = new URL(req.url, `http://${req.headers.host}`);
const id = parsed.searchParams.get('id');
examples.explanation: url.parse is deprecated and has known parsing vulnerabilities. Use the WHATWG URL constructor.

examples.bad: const cipher = crypto.createCipher('aes-256-cbc', password);
examples.explanation: createCipher uses an insecure key derivation. Always use createCipheriv with a proper key derivation function and random IV.

patterns.description: Deprecated Express.js APIs and patterns

detection_strategy.method: Detect deprecated Express middleware and method usage.

known_deprecations.notes: body-parser is built into Express since 4.16. No separate install needed.
app.del was an alias removed in Express 4.
Note the capitalization difference.
req.param() searched params, query, and body which was confusing. Be explicit.
Use the multer package for multipart form handling.
Use if (process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production') instead.

examples.good:
app.use(express.json());
app.use(express.urlencoded({ extended: true }));
examples.explanation: body-parser is built into Express since 4.16. No separate package needed.

patterns.description: Deprecated Python patterns from AI training data

detection_strategy.method: Scan for deprecated Python patterns and standard library usage.

known_deprecations.notes: AI occasionally generates Python 2 print statements.
Use X | None syntax instead of Optional[X] in Python 3.10+.
Use X | Y syntax instead of Union[X, Y] in Python 3.10+.
Use built-in generics: list[int], dict[str, int], etc.
Use async def instead of @asyncio.coroutine with yield from.
ABCs moved to collections.abc submodule.
distutils was removed in Python 3.12. Use setuptools.
Use TestLoader methods instead.
Use importlib for programmatic imports.
cgi module was removed. Use modern alternatives.
pkg_resources is slow and deprecated. Use importlib.resources and importlib.metadata.

examples.bad:
from typing import List, Dict, Optional
def process(items: List[Dict[str, Optional[int]]]) -> None:
examples.good: def process(items: list[dict[str, int | None]]) -> None:
examples.explanation: In Python 3.10+, use built-in generics (list, dict) and X | None instead of typing.Optional. In 3.9, you can use built-in generics but not the | syntax.

examples.bad: from collections import MutableMapping
examples.good: from collections.abc import MutableMapping
examples.explanation: Abstract base classes were removed from the top-level collections module in Python 3.10. Import from collections.abc.

patterns.description: Unsafe type assertions that bypass TypeScript's type system

detection_strategy.method: Scan for 'as' assertions, 'any' casts, non-null assertions (!), and @ts-ignore/@ts-expect-error comments.

heuristics: Type assertion used instead of proper type narrowing
as any used to silence type errors
Non-null assertion (!) on values that could genuinely be null
@ts-ignore used instead of fixing the type error
Double assertion (as unknown as T) to force incompatible types

known_patterns.description: Casting to 'any' to bypass type checking
known_patterns.notes: Almost always a sign of a type error being suppressed rather than fixed.

known_patterns.description: Double assertion through unknown (as unknown as T)
known_patterns.notes: Double assertion forces incompatible types. This almost always indicates a design problem.

known_patterns.description: Non-null assertion on .find() result
known_patterns.notes: .find() returns T | undefined. Using ! assumes it always finds a result.

known_patterns.description: Non-null assertion on DOM queries
known_patterns.notes: DOM queries can return null. Using ! can cause runtime errors.

known_patterns.description: Type assertion on API response without validation
known_patterns.notes: API responses should be validated (e.g., with zod) not asserted.

known_patterns.description: @ts-ignore without explanation
known_patterns.notes: Use @ts-expect-error with a comment explaining why the error is expected.

known_patterns.description: Type assertion on event objects
known_patterns.notes: Event handlers should use proper event types from React or DOM typings.

known_patterns.description: Using 'as const' on mutable objects to fake immutability
known_patterns.notes: 'as const' only affects type inference, not runtime behavior. Object is still mutable.

known_patterns.description: Using any as a generic type parameter
known_patterns.notes: Passing any as a generic parameter defeats the purpose of generics.

known_patterns.description: Asserting return type instead of letting TypeScript infer
known_patterns.notes: If you need to assert the return type, the function logic likely doesn't match the expected type.

examples.bad:
const data = await fetch('/api/users').then(r => r.json()) as User[];
return data.map(u => u.name);
examples.explanation: Type assertions on API responses provide zero runtime safety. The API could return anything. Validate with a schema library like zod.

examples.bad:
const element = document.getElementById('root')!;
ReactDOM.createRoot(element).render(<App />);
examples.explanation: Non-null assertion (!) on DOM queries suppresses a real possibility of null. Add an explicit check with a descriptive error.

examples.bad:
const user = users.find(u => u.id === id) as User;
sendEmail(user.email);
examples.explanation: .find() returns T | undefined. Asserting away the undefined case hides a potential runtime error when the item is not found.

examples.bad: (config as any).secretField = value;
examples.good: // Fix the Config type to include secretField, or use a properly typed extension
examples.explanation: 'as any' silences the type error but introduces a type hole. Fix the underlying type instead.

patterns.description: Python type annotation mistakes AI commonly makes

detection_strategy.method: Analyze type annotations for common mistakes in Python typing.

known_patterns.description: Mutable default argument in function definition
known_patterns.notes: Classic Python gotcha. Mutable defaults are shared between calls. Use None as default and create inside function.

known_patterns.description: Using Optional incorrectly (Optional[X, Y] instead of Optional[X])
known_patterns.notes: Optional takes exactly one type argument. Optional[X] means X | None. For multiple types use Union.

known_patterns.description: Using type() instead of isinstance() for type checking
known_patterns.notes: type() doesn't account for inheritance. Use isinstance() for type checking.

known_patterns.description: Class variable annotated as instance variable in dataclasses
known_patterns.notes: In dataclasses, plain annotations become instance fields. Use ClassVar[] for class-level attributes.

examples.explanation: Mutable default arguments are shared across all calls to the function. Each call that modifies the default list affects subsequent calls.

patterns.description: Missing error handling in async operations

detection_strategy.method: Identify async operations without try-catch, .catch(), or error callbacks.

heuristics: fetch() calls without checking response.ok
await without try-catch in request handlers
Promise chains without .catch()
Event listeners on error-emitting objects without error handler
JSON.parse without try-catch

known_patterns.description: fetch() without checking response.ok or response.status
known_patterns.notes: fetch does NOT throw on HTTP errors (404, 500). Only network failures throw. Must check response.ok.

known_patterns.description: JSON.parse on untrusted input without error handling
known_patterns.notes: JSON.parse throws SyntaxError on malformed input. Always wrap in try-catch when parsing external data.

known_patterns.description: Promise chain without .catch() or enclosing try-catch
known_patterns.notes: Unhandled promise rejections crash Node.js. Always handle the rejection case.

known_patterns.description: EventEmitter or Stream without 'error' handler
known_patterns.notes: Unhandled 'error' events on EventEmitters crash the process.

known_patterns.description: Express async route handler without error handling
known_patterns.notes: Express does not catch errors from async handlers. Must wrap in try-catch or use express-async-errors.

known_patterns.description: File system operations without error handling
known_patterns.notes: File operations can fail for many reasons (permissions, missing files, disk full). Always handle errors.

known_patterns.description: Database operations without error handling
known_patterns.notes: Database operations can fail due to connection issues, constraint violations, timeouts, etc.

examples.explanation: fetch does not throw on HTTP error status codes. A 404 or 500 response will happily proceed to .json(), which may then fail with a confusing parse error instead of a clear HTTP error.
Express does not catch async errors. If the database query throws, the error is unhandled and the client hangs. Wrap in try-catch and call next(err).

examples.bad: const config = JSON.parse(userInput);
examples.explanation: JSON.parse throws SyntaxError on malformed input. When parsing user input or external data, always wrap in try-catch.

patterns.description: Missing error handling in Python code

detection_strategy.method: Identify operations that commonly fail without try-except blocks.

known_patterns.description: Bare except clause that catches everything including SystemExit and KeyboardInterrupt
known_patterns.notes: Always specify the exception type. Use 'except Exception:' at minimum.

known_patterns.description: except with only pass (silently swallowing errors)
known_patterns.notes: Silently ignoring exceptions hides bugs. At minimum, log the error.

known_patterns.description: open() without context manager or error handling
known_patterns.notes: Always use 'with open(...) as f:' to ensure the file is closed, even on error.

known_patterns.description: requests.get/post without error handling
known_patterns.notes: HTTP requests can fail with ConnectionError, Timeout, etc. Use try-except or response.raise_for_status().

known_patterns.description: json.loads on untrusted input without error handling
known_patterns.notes: json.loads raises JSONDecodeError on malformed input.

known_patterns.description: Dict key access without .get() or KeyError handling
known_patterns.notes: dict['key'] raises KeyError. Use dict.get('key', default) for potentially missing keys.

examples.explanation: Bare 'except: pass' silently swallows ALL errors, including SystemExit and KeyboardInterrupt, making debugging impossible.

examples.bad:
f = open('data.txt', 'r')
content = f.read()
f.close()
examples.good:
with open('data.txt', 'r') as f:
    content = f.read()
examples.explanation: Without a context manager, if f.read() raises an exception, f.close() is never called, leaking the file descriptor.

patterns.description: Convention-violating error handling detection heuristics

detection_strategy.method: Analyze existing error handling patterns in the project and flag new code that violates established conventions.

convention_detection_heuristics.description: Project defines custom error classes but AI uses generic Error
convention_detection_heuristics.detection_method: Scan for class definitions extending Error/Exception. If found, flag new code that throws plain Error.
convention_detection_heuristics.notes: If the project has AppError, NotFoundError, etc., new code should use them.

convention_detection_heuristics.description: Project uses Result/Either pattern but AI throws exceptions
convention_detection_heuristics.detection_method: Check for Result<T, E> or Either type usage. If found, flag new code that throws instead of returning Result.
convention_detection_heuristics.notes: Some projects use functional error handling. AI defaults to throw/try-catch.

convention_detection_heuristics.description: Project has React error boundaries but AI doesn't throw renderable errors
convention_detection_heuristics.detection_method: Check for ErrorBoundary components. If found, verify that errors thrown in components will be caught.
convention_detection_heuristics.notes: Error boundaries only catch errors during rendering, not in event handlers or async code.

convention_detection_heuristics.description: Project has a global error handler middleware but AI adds redundant try-catch
convention_detection_heuristics.detection_method: Check for Express error middleware (app.use with 4 params). If found, AI code may not need individual try-catch.
convention_detection_heuristics.notes: If the project uses express-async-errors + global handler, individual try-catch may be unnecessary noise.

convention_detection_heuristics.description: Project uses a logging library but AI uses console.error
convention_detection_heuristics.detection_method: Check for logger imports (winston, pino, bunyan, log4js). If found, flag console.error in new code.
convention_detection_heuristics.notes: AI defaults to console.error which bypasses structured logging.

convention_detection_heuristics.description: Project uses error monitoring (Sentry, Datadog, etc.) but AI doesn't report errors
convention_detection_heuristics.detection_method: Check for Sentry/monitoring imports. If found, flag error catches that don't report.
convention_detection_heuristics.notes: Caught errors should be reported to the monitoring service.

examples.bad: throw new Error('User not found');
examples.good: throw new NotFoundError('User not found');
examples.explanation: The project defines custom error classes for consistency and error handling middleware. Using plain Error bypasses error categorization.
The project uses pino for structured logging and Sentry for error monitoring. console.error bypasses both systems.

patterns.description: Incorrect regex patterns AI commonly generates

detection_strategy.method: Parse regex literals and validate them against known incorrect patterns.

known_patterns.description: Overly simple email regex that rejects valid emails or accepts invalid ones
known_patterns.notes: Email validation is notoriously hard. Simple regexes miss valid addresses (e.g., with +, subdomains) or accept invalid ones. Use a library or just check for @ and send a confirmation.

known_patterns.description: URL regex that misses valid URLs or has catastrophic backtracking
known_patterns.notes: URL validation via regex is complex. Use the URL constructor for validation instead.

known_patterns.description: Using '.' (any char) when literal dot was intended
known_patterns.notes: AI often forgets to escape dots in regexes for IP addresses, domain names, etc.

known_patterns.description: Regex with nested quantifiers that causes exponential backtracking
known_patterns.notes: Patterns like (a+)+ or (a|a)+ cause catastrophic backtracking. Can be exploited for ReDoS attacks.

known_patterns.description: Phone number regex that only works for one country
known_patterns.notes: AI often generates US-only phone regexes. Use a library like libphonenumber for international support.

examples.bad: const isValidEmail = (email) => /^[a-zA-Z0-9]+@[a-zA-Z0-9]+\.[a-zA-Z]{2,}$/.test(email);
examples.explanation: The regex rejects valid emails with +, dots in local part, hyphens in domain, long TLDs, etc. Email validation via regex is almost always wrong. Do a basic check and send a confirmation email.

examples.bad:
const pattern = /^(a+)+$/;
pattern.test(userInput);
examples.good:
const pattern = /^a+$/;
pattern.test(userInput);
examples.explanation: Nested quantifiers (a+)+ cause catastrophic backtracking on inputs like 'aaaaaaaaaaaaaab'. This can be exploited to freeze the server (ReDoS attack).

patterns.description: Race conditions and concurrency bugs AI commonly introduces

detection_strategy.method: Identify patterns that are vulnerable to race conditions.

known_patterns.description: Check-then-act pattern without atomicity (TOCTOU)
known_patterns.notes: if (exists) then write, if (available) then reserve -- the check and action are not atomic.

known_patterns.description: Read-modify-write without locking or atomic operations
known_patterns.notes: counter = await getCount(); counter++; await setCount(counter); -- not atomic.

known_patterns.description: Shared mutable variable in concurrent callback contexts
known_patterns.notes: let result; promises.forEach(async p => { result = await p; }); -- result is overwritten by whoever finishes last.

known_patterns.description: Multiple state updates without batching in React
known_patterns.notes: In React 17 and below, state updates outside React event handlers are not batched. React 18 batches them automatically.

examples.explanation: Between the findUser check and createUser call, another request could create the same user. Use database-level uniqueness constraints and handle the conflict.

patterns.description: Non-existent CSS/Tailwind classes AI commonly generates

detection_strategy.method: Check Tailwind class names against the actual Tailwind CSS configuration and class list.

known_hallucinations.notes: Tailwind uses font-bold, not text-bold.
No flex-middle class. Use items-center for vertical centering in a flex container.
No flex-between. Use justify-between.
Use the truncate utility which sets overflow, text-overflow, and white-space together.
No align-center in Tailwind. Use text-center for text alignment or items-center for flex alignment.
Tailwind uses short-form: flex, block, grid, etc.
In Tailwind v3+, use the color/opacity syntax: bg-black/50 instead of separate bg-opacity utilities.
Tailwind uses shadow-sm as the smallest named shadow. shadow-xs was added in v4 only.
Smallest named border-radius is rounded-sm.
text-primary only works if 'primary' is defined in the Tailwind config theme. Not a default class.

examples.bad: <div className="flex flex-between text-bold">
examples.good: <div className="flex justify-between font-bold">
examples.explanation: flex-between and text-bold are not valid Tailwind classes. Use justify-between and font-bold.

patterns.description: Security-sensitive logic errors AI commonly introduces

detection_strategy.method: Identify patterns that create security vulnerabilities.

known_patterns.description: String concatenation or template literals in SQL queries
known_patterns.notes: AI often interpolates user input directly into SQL strings instead of using parameterized queries.

known_patterns.description: Setting inner HTML with user-controlled content
known_patterns.notes: AI often sets innerHTML without sanitizing input, creating XSS vulnerabilities.

known_patterns.description: Using Math.random() for security-sensitive operations
known_patterns.notes: Math.random() is not cryptographically secure. Use crypto.randomUUID() or crypto.getRandomValues().

known_patterns.description: Hardcoded secrets, API keys, or passwords in source code
known_patterns.notes: AI often includes placeholder secrets that look like real ones. Always use environment variables.

known_patterns.description: File path constructed from user input without sanitization
known_patterns.notes: User input in file paths enables directory traversal attacks (../../etc/passwd).

known_patterns.description: Dynamic code execution with user-controlled input via Function constructor or similar mechanisms
known_patterns.notes: AI sometimes uses dynamic code execution for flexibility instead of safer alternatives like lookup tables or strategy patterns.

known_patterns.description: Redirect URL from user input without validation
known_patterns.notes: User-controlled redirect URLs enable phishing attacks.

known_patterns.description: CORS origin set to * with credentials
known_patterns.notes: Wildcard CORS origin with credentials is insecure and actually blocked by browsers. AI generates it anyway.

examples.bad: const users = await db.query(`SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '${req.query.name}'`);
examples.good: const users = await db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = $1', [req.query.name]);
examples.explanation: String interpolation in SQL queries enables SQL injection. Always use parameterized queries.

examples.bad: const token = Math.random().toString(36).slice(2);
examples.explanation: Math.random() is predictable and not suitable for tokens, session IDs, or any security-sensitive values.

examples.bad:
const filePath = path.join('/uploads', req.params.filename);
const content = fs.readFileSync(filePath);
examples.explanation: Without sanitization, a filename like '../../etc/passwd' allows reading arbitrary files on the server.

patterns.description: Deprecated or outdated web platform APIs

detection_strategy.method: Scan for deprecated Web API usage.

known_deprecations.notes: document.write can cause issues with parsing and is blocked in some contexts.
document.all is falsy despite being an object. A legacy quirk.
Use the Fetch API or a library like axios. XHR is legacy.
Deprecated and returns misleading values. Use feature detection.
Use event.key instead of event.keyCode for keyboard events.
Use event.preventDefault() instead.
window.event is non-standard. Accept the event as a handler parameter.
AI often uses localStorage.clear() for logout which wipes all stored data including preferences.

examples.explanation: keyCode is deprecated. Use the .key property which returns readable strings like 'Enter', 'Escape', etc.

patterns.description: Environment and configuration mistakes AI commonly makes

detection_strategy.method: Check for common environment variable and configuration mistakes.

known_patterns.description: Using environment variable without fallback or validation
known_patterns.notes: process.env values are always string | undefined. AI often uses them without checking if they exist.

known_patterns.description: Checking environment variable as boolean (it's always a string)
known_patterns.notes: process.env.FOO === true is always false because env vars are strings. Check === 'true' instead.

known_patterns.description: Comparing environment variable as number without parsing
known_patterns.notes: Environment variables are strings. '10' > '9' is false (string comparison). Parse to number first.

known_patterns.description: Accessing server-only environment variable on the client
known_patterns.notes: In Next.js, only NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefixed env vars are available on the client. AI often uses server env vars in client components.

known_patterns.description: Hardcoded localhost URL that won't work in production
known_patterns.notes: AI often hardcodes localhost URLs for API endpoints instead of using environment variables.

examples.explanation: Environment variables are always strings. Comparing to boolean true always returns false. Compare to the string 'true'.

examples.bad: const API_URL = 'http://localhost:3000/api';
examples.good: const API_URL = process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL || 'http://localhost:3000/api';
examples.explanation: Hardcoded localhost URLs break in any non-local environment. Use environment variables with localhost as the fallback.

patterns.description: Incorrect async patterns that cause silent failures, data loss, or race conditions

detection_strategy.method: Analyze async function usage for patterns that violate JavaScript's concurrency model: await in forEach, missing await, incorrect parallelization, unguarded state updates, and improper cleanup in useEffect.

heuristics: await used inside .forEach() callback - forEach ignores the returned promise
async function called without await and return value used as resolved data
Promise.all used on operations that must execute in sequence (e.g., migrations, dependent API calls)
async function passed to useEffect without an inner IIFE or separate function, and without cleanup
Shared mutable variable updated from multiple concurrent async operations without guarding

known_patterns.description: Using await inside Array.forEach - the callback returns a promise that forEach discards, so the loop does not wait for completion
known_patterns.notes: forEach returns void and ignores the promise returned by the async callback. Code after the forEach runs immediately. Use for...of for sequential execution or Promise.all(arr.map(...)) for parallel.

known_patterns.description: Calling an async function without await and using the return value as if it were resolved
known_patterns.notes: The return value is a Promise object, not the resolved value. Comparisons like 'if (fetchUser(id))' are always truthy because Promise objects are truthy. Assignments like 'const user = getUser(id)' store a Promise, not a User.

known_patterns.description: Using Promise.all on operations that depend on each other's results or ordering
known_patterns.notes: Promise.all runs all promises concurrently. If operation B depends on operation A's result, or if operations must run in a specific order (e.g., database migrations, sequential API calls with side effects), use a for...of loop with await instead.

known_patterns.description: Passing an async function directly to useEffect or performing async work in useEffect without cancellation on unmount
known_patterns.notes: useEffect expects its callback to return void or a cleanup function. An async function returns a Promise, which useEffect cannot use as cleanup. Additionally, without an AbortController or cancelled flag, the async operation may try to update state on an unmounted component.

known_patterns.description: Unguarded state updates from async operations that can race against each other
known_patterns.notes: When multiple async operations can update the same state (e.g., search-as-you-type, rapid button clicks), earlier requests that resolve later overwrite the results of newer requests. Use AbortController, request IDs, or debouncing to guard against this.

examples.explanation: forEach does not await async callbacks. It fires all promises immediately and moves on. The console.log runs before any item is processed. Use for...of for sequential or Promise.all(arr.map(...)) for parallel.

examples.bad:
const user = getUser(userId);
console.log(user.name);
examples.good:
const user = await getUser(userId);
console.log(user.name);
examples.explanation: getUser is async and returns a Promise. Without await, 'user' is a Promise object, and 'user.name' is undefined. The code silently produces wrong results without throwing.
createProfile depends on newUser.id from createUser, so they cannot run in parallel. Promise.all would start createProfile before createUser finishes, using an undefined userId.

useEffect cannot accept an async function because it expects void or a cleanup function, not a Promise. Wrap async work in a named inner function, and provide cancellation via AbortController so unmounted components do not get state updates.
Without a cancellation guard, if the user types fast and query changes rapidly, an older slower request can resolve after a newer one, overwriting correct results with stale data.

patterns.description: Violations of React's rules and common misuse of React APIs that cause infinite loops, stale data, or runtime errors

detection_strategy.method: Analyze React component code for hook rule violations, incorrect state management, Server/Client Component confusion, and unsafe rendering patterns.

heuristics: useState setter called directly in the render body (outside useEffect, event handler, or callback) causing infinite re-renders
useEffect with missing values in the dependency array that are referenced inside the effect body
Hooks called inside if/else blocks, loops, or early returns
Server Component (no 'use client' directive) using useState, useEffect, useRef, or other client hooks
List rendering with missing key prop or using array index as key when items can be reordered, inserted, or deleted
dangerouslySetInnerHTML used with dynamic user-provided content without sanitization

known_patterns.description: Calling a useState setter directly in the component body causes an infinite re-render loop
known_patterns.notes: When setState is called during render, React re-renders the component, which calls setState again, creating an infinite loop. Move state updates to useEffect, event handlers, or callbacks. The exception is the rare 'state update during render' pattern for derived state, which requires a conditional guard.

known_patterns.description: useEffect references values from the component scope but does not list them in the dependency array, causing stale closure bugs
known_patterns.notes: When a value referenced inside useEffect is not in the dependency array, the effect captures a stale snapshot of that value. The effect runs with outdated data even when the value changes. The exhaustive-deps ESLint rule catches this, but AI-generated code often has it disabled or ignored.

known_patterns.description: Hooks called inside conditional blocks, loops, or after early returns
known_patterns.notes: React relies on hooks being called in the same order every render. Conditional hook calls change the order, corrupting the internal hook state. This causes unpredictable bugs and crashes. The 'Rules of Hooks' ESLint plugin catches this, but AI sometimes generates it when trying to optimize or handle edge cases.

known_patterns.description: Using client-only hooks (useState, useEffect, useRef, useContext, etc.) in a Server Component
known_patterns.notes: In Next.js App Router, components without 'use client' are Server Components by default. Server Components run on the server and cannot use React hooks that depend on client-side state or lifecycle. This causes a build error. Add 'use client' at the top of the file, or extract the interactive part into a separate Client Component.

known_patterns.description: List items rendered with .map() without a key prop, or using the array index as key when items can be reordered
known_patterns.notes: Missing keys cause React to re-mount all items on every change. Index keys cause React to associate state with the wrong items when the list is reordered, filtered, or items are inserted/removed. Use a stable unique ID from the data.

known_patterns.description: Using dangerouslySetInnerHTML with content that includes user input or dynamic data without sanitization
known_patterns.notes: dangerouslySetInnerHTML renders raw HTML. If the content includes user input, attackers can inject scripts (XSS). Always sanitize with a library like DOMPurify before rendering. If the content is a static string literal, it is safe.

examples.explanation: Calling setCount during render triggers a re-render, which calls setCount again, creating an infinite loop. Move state updates to event handlers or useEffect.

The effect uses userId but does not list it in the dependency array. When userId changes (e.g., navigating to a different user), the effect does not re-run, and the component displays stale data from the previous user.
Hooks must be called in the same order every render. When showExtra changes from true to false, the hook call order changes, corrupting React's internal state for all subsequent hooks.
In Next.js App Router, components without 'use client' are Server Components and cannot use useState or other client hooks. Add 'use client' at the top of any component that needs interactivity.

Using array index as key causes React to associate component state with the wrong items when todos are reordered, deleted, or inserted. A todo's checkbox state would appear on a different todo. Use a stable unique ID from the data.
If htmlContent comes from user input or an API, it can contain script tags or event handlers that execute arbitrary JavaScript (XSS). Always sanitize with DOMPurify or a similar library.

patterns.description: Common Next.js mistakes where AI confuses router paradigms, uses wrong imports, or misses version-specific API changes

detection_strategy.method: Detect Pages Router patterns used in App Router files, wrong router imports, synchronous usage of async request APIs in Next.js 15+, unnecessary client-side data fetching in Server Components, and missing 'use client' directives.

heuristics: getServerSideProps, getStaticProps, or getStaticPaths exported from a file inside the app/ directory
useRouter imported from next/router in a file inside the app/ directory
cookies(), headers(), or draftMode() called without await in a Next.js 15+ project
Client-side data fetching hooks (useEffect + fetch, useSWR, useQuery) used in a Server Component when server-side fetch would suffice
Component using useState, useEffect, or other client hooks but missing the 'use client' directive at the top of the file

known_patterns.description: Using Pages Router data fetching functions (getServerSideProps, getStaticProps, getStaticPaths) in the app/ directory
known_patterns.context_check: Only flag if the file path contains /app/. These exports are valid in /pages/.
known_patterns.notes: In the App Router, data fetching is done directly in the Server Component body using async/await or via generateStaticParams. The Pages Router export functions are silently ignored in app/ -- they do not cause a build error but they do not work either, causing confusing behavior.

known_patterns.description: Importing useRouter from next/router instead of next/navigation in App Router files
known_patterns.context_check: Only flag if the file path contains /app/. next/router is correct in /pages/.
known_patterns.notes: next/router's useRouter has .query, .asPath, .pathname, etc. next/navigation's useRouter only has push, replace, back, forward, refresh, and prefetch. Using the wrong import causes runtime errors or missing properties.

known_patterns.description: Calling cookies(), headers(), or draftMode() without await in Next.js 15+
known_patterns.context_check: Check the next version in package.json. Only flag if version >= 15.
known_patterns.notes: In Next.js 15, cookies(), headers(), and draftMode() became async and return Promises. Calling them without await returns a Promise object instead of the actual cookies/headers. This is a breaking change from Next.js 14. Code like 'const cookieStore = cookies()' must become 'const cookieStore = await cookies()'.

known_patterns.description: Using client-side data fetching (useEffect + fetch, useSWR, useQuery) in a Server Component when server-side fetch would be simpler and faster
known_patterns.notes: Server Components can fetch data directly with async/await in the component body. Using useEffect + fetch or data fetching hooks adds unnecessary client-side JavaScript, causes loading states, and prevents streaming. This is not a bug but a significant anti-pattern that degrades performance and UX.

known_patterns.description: Component file uses client-only hooks or browser APIs but is missing the 'use client' directive
known_patterns.notes: In the App Router, all components are Server Components by default. If a component uses useState, useEffect, useRef, useContext, onClick handlers, or browser APIs like window/document, it must have 'use client' at the top. Missing this directive causes a build error with a message about hooks not being available in Server Components.

examples.explanation: getServerSideProps is a Pages Router pattern and does not work in the App Router. In App Router, Server Components are async by default -- just fetch data directly in the component body.

In the App Router, useRouter must be imported from next/navigation, not next/router. The next/navigation version has no .query property. Use useSearchParams() for query parameters or useParams() for dynamic route segments.

In Next.js 15, cookies() is async and returns a Promise. Without await, cookieStore is a Promise object, and .get('theme') returns undefined. This is a breaking change from Next.js 14 where cookies() was synchronous.

This page does not need any client-side interactivity -- it just fetches and displays data. As a Server Component, it can fetch data directly with async/await, avoiding the client-side JavaScript bundle, loading states, and the waterfall of client-server round trips.

This component uses useState and onClick, which require client-side JavaScript. Without 'use client' at the top, Next.js treats it as a Server Component and the build fails with an error about hooks not being available.

patterns.description: Database access anti-patterns that cause performance issues, data corruption, or security vulnerabilities

detection_strategy.method: Analyze database query patterns for N+1 loops, raw SQL with string interpolation, missing transactions for multi-step operations, non-deterministic findFirst queries, and missing connection pool configuration.

heuristics: Database query inside a for/forEach/map loop (N+1 pattern)
SQL query string built with template literals or string concatenation containing user input
Multiple related database writes (create + update, transfer between accounts) without a transaction
Prisma findFirst without a unique constraint or orderBy clause, giving non-deterministic results
Database client created without explicit connection pool size, timeout, or retry configuration

known_patterns.description: Database query executed inside a loop, causing N+1 queries instead of a single batched query
known_patterns.notes: For N items, this executes N+1 queries (1 to fetch the list, N to fetch related data). Causes severe performance degradation as N grows. Use include/join/eager loading, or batch queries with IN clauses.

known_patterns.description: SQL query built with template literals that interpolate user-controlled values
known_patterns.notes: Template literals in SQL queries allow attackers to inject arbitrary SQL. Use parameterized queries ($1, ?, :param), tagged template literals (Prisma's Prisma.sql`...`), or query builder methods that handle escaping.

known_patterns.description: Multiple related database writes that should be atomic but are executed independently
known_patterns.notes: If any write in a multi-step sequence fails, the database is left in an inconsistent state. Examples: transferring money between accounts (debit + credit), creating a user and their profile, updating inventory and creating an order. Use database transactions to ensure all-or-nothing semantics.

known_patterns.description: Using Prisma findFirst without a unique constraint in the where clause and without an orderBy, giving non-deterministic results
known_patterns.notes: findFirst returns the first matching record, but without orderBy the 'first' record is database-engine-dependent and non-deterministic. If you expect exactly one result, use findUnique with a unique field. If you want the latest or earliest, add orderBy.

known_patterns.description: Database client or pool created without configuring connection limits, timeouts, or idle settings
known_patterns.notes: Default connection pool settings are rarely appropriate for production. Without limits, the application can exhaust database connections under load. Without timeouts, queries can hang indefinitely. Without idle settings, connections are leaked. Set pool size, connection timeout, idle timeout, and maximum lifetime.

examples.explanation: The bad example executes N+1 queries: 1 for all users, then 1 per user for their posts. With 100 users, that is 101 database round trips. The good example uses a single query with an include (JOIN) to fetch everything at once.

examples.bad:
const userId = req.params.id;
const user = await db.query(`SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = '${userId}'`);
examples.good:
const userId = req.params.id;
const user = await db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1', [userId]);
examples.explanation: Interpolating userId directly into the SQL string allows SQL injection. If userId is "'; DROP TABLE users; --", the table is deleted. Parameterized queries send the value separately from the query string, preventing injection.

Without a transaction, if the second update fails (e.g., network error), money is deducted from the sender but never added to the receiver. A transaction ensures both operations succeed or both are rolled back.

Without orderBy, findFirst returns whichever record the database engine happens to find first, which can vary between executions, replicas, or after vacuuming. If you want the latest pending order, specify orderBy.

Without pool configuration, the default max connections (usually 10) may be too low for production, and there are no timeouts to recover from hung connections. Configure max pool size based on your database plan and expected concurrency.

patterns.description: HTTP API design anti-patterns that cause integration issues, security vulnerabilities, and scalability problems

detection_strategy.method: Analyze API route handlers for incorrect status codes, missing headers, sensitive data exposure, missing pagination, and missing rate limiting.

heuristics: Error responses sent with HTTP 200 status code instead of appropriate 4xx/5xx codes
Response missing Content-Type header or sending JSON without application/json
Sensitive data (tokens, passwords, secrets) included in URL query parameters instead of request body or headers
List endpoint that returns all records without limit/offset or cursor-based pagination
Public-facing endpoint without rate limiting, allowing abuse and denial of service

known_patterns.description: Returning HTTP 200 OK for error responses, with the error encoded in the response body
known_patterns.notes: AI often returns { success: false, error: 'message' } with a 200 status. This breaks standard HTTP client error handling, caching, and monitoring. Use 400 for client errors, 401 for authentication failures, 403 for authorization failures, 404 for missing resources, 409 for conflicts, and 500 for server errors.

known_patterns.description: API response missing Content-Type header, causing clients to guess the response format
known_patterns.notes: Most frameworks set Content-Type automatically when using .json() or equivalent, but manual response construction (new Response, res.send with a string) may omit it. Missing Content-Type can cause parsing failures in strict HTTP clients.

known_patterns.description: Tokens, passwords, API keys, or other sensitive data passed as URL query parameters
known_patterns.notes: URL parameters are logged in server access logs, browser history, referrer headers, and proxy logs. Sensitive data must be sent in the request body (POST) or Authorization header. This applies to both API-to-API communication and user-facing endpoints.

known_patterns.description: List endpoint that returns all matching records without pagination, causing memory and performance issues at scale
known_patterns.notes: Without pagination, a list endpoint loads all records into memory. With 10 records in development this works fine; with 10 million in production it crashes the server. Always add limit/offset (offset-based) or cursor (cursor-based) pagination.

known_patterns.description: Public-facing API endpoint without rate limiting, allowing abuse
known_patterns.notes: Endpoints like login, signup, password reset, and public APIs should have rate limiting to prevent brute force attacks, credential stuffing, and denial of service. AI rarely adds rate limiting because it is typically middleware-level infrastructure. Check if the project has a global rate limiter before flagging individual endpoints.

examples.explanation: Returning 200 with { success: false } means HTTP clients, load balancers, and monitoring tools treat the error as a successful response. Use proper HTTP status codes so the entire stack understands the response semantics.

The token and new password are in URL query parameters, which are logged in server access logs, browser history, and referrer headers. Sensitive data must be sent in the request body via POST. Also note the method change from GET to POST -- password reset is a state-changing operation.

Without pagination, this endpoint loads ALL users into memory and sends them in a single response. With millions of users this exhausts server memory and produces an unusably large response. Always paginate list endpoints with a capped limit.

Without rate limiting, attackers can brute-force passwords by making thousands of login attempts per second. A rate limiter (10 attempts per 15 minutes) stops brute force while allowing legitimate users to retry after typos.
Two issues: the API key is in URL parameters (logged everywhere), and the unauthorized response returns 200. Move the key to the Authorization header and return 401 for authentication failures.

usage_notes.detection_regex_language: Most detection_regex patterns use JavaScript regex syntax. Python consumers should adapt escaping as needed.
usage_notes.confidence_scores: Confidence scores (0.0-1.0) indicate how likely a regex match is to be a true positive. Lower scores indicate more false positives and may need additional context.
usage_notes.severity_override: Some individual patterns have a severity_override that supersedes their category's default severity.
usage_notes.context_sensitive: Many patterns are context-sensitive. For example, getServerSideProps is only deprecated in the app/ directory, not in pages/. Detection tools should account for file location and project configuration.
